did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (ConvNeXtV2, ResNet18, EfficientNet, and VGG16) to perform multiclass skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. The study involved training these CNN architectures, evaluating their performance using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and using Grad-CAM for explainability to assist in diagnosing skin cancer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work comparing four deep learning models (ConvNeXtV2, ResNet18, EfficientNet, and VGG16) on the HAM10000 dataset for multiclass skin lesion classification. It details specific methodologies including data augmentation, training with AdamW optimizer, and evaluation using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, along with Grad-CAM for explainability. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICECONF), it reports new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Explainable Deep Learning Model Leveraging ConvNeXtV2 for Multiclass Skin Lesion Classification
journal: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Knowledge Discovery in Concurrent Engineering (ICECONF)
brief_summary: This study evaluated four deep learning models—ConvNeXtV2, ResNet18, EfficientNet, and VGG16—for multiclass skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The models were trained with data augmentation and the AdamW optimizer to address class imbalance and improve generalization. ConvNeXtV2 achieved the highest performance, attaining 88% accuracy and an F1-score of 0.87, with particular strength in melanoma detection. The results demonstrated that ConvNeXtV2 outperformed the other architectures in handling complex patterns and rare lesion types. Additionally, Grad-CAM was employed to provide visual explanations of model predictions, enhancing interpretability for clinical trust. The findings suggest that deep learning, particularly ConvNeXtV2, holds significant potential for assisting physicians in the early and accurate diagnosis of skin cancer.

The HAM10000 dataset contains 7 classes total. The paper explicitly lists 6 types (Actinic Keratosis, Atypical Moles, Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma). The 7th class is not explicitly named in the provided text chunks, but standard HAM10000 classes include Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, and Vascular lesions. The paper mentions classifying 'benign and malignant lesions' generally.

all_task_types.target: Multiclass skin lesion categories (Actinic Keratosis, Atypical Moles, Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Benign Keratosis variants) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'multiclass skin lesion classification'. The models (ConvNeXtV2, ResNet18, EfficientNet, VGG16) predict discrete diagnostic categories using cross-entropy loss. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for each class. While Grad-CAM is used for explainability, it does not change the primary task type from classification.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A well-established and reliable resource for AI dermatology studies containing over 10,000 high-quality images of skin lesions. It is a public dermoscopic image dataset.
classes_used: Benign Keratosis (and other benign variants implied by the 7-class structure)

use.training: Used for training the ConvNeXtV2, ResNet18, EfficientNet, and VGG16 models with data augmentation to address class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for monitoring validation data during training to adjust hyperparameters and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure class balance in the training, validation, and testing phases of the dataset.
To evaluate model performance across all classes consistently, handling class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model comparisons were based on descriptive performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

ConvNeXtV2.Notes: Best overall results; highest accuracy and F1-score; notable excelling in melanoma detection.

EfficientNet.Notes: Closely follows ConvNeXtV2; balanced and consistent results; reliable choice for classification tasks.

ResNet18.Notes: Strong accuracy in detecting benign nevi (937 or NV) with precision 0.89 and recall 0.97; poor sensitivity to melanoma.

VGG16.Notes: Moderate overall performance; lower accuracy and F1-score compared to other models; handles all classes consistently well despite lower absolute scores.

HAM10000.notes: ConvNeXtV2 outperformed ResNet18, EfficientNet, and VGG16. EfficientNet was the second best performer. ResNet18 performed well on common lesions but poorly on rare ones like melanoma. VGG16 showed competitive results but was behind the top performers.

architecture_details.ConvNeXtV2: State-of-the-art model combining convolution and attention mechanisms; described as the most complex model with optimized layers.
architecture_details.ResNet18: Simple model leveraging shortcut connections to allow for easier training of deeper layers.
architecture_details.EfficientNet: Small and portable model that scales depth, width, and input in a balanced way.
architecture_details.VGG16: Practical model with focus on speed and limited-resource characteristics, ideal for mobile.

Data augmentation including vertical and horizontal flipping, rotation, and brightness/color adjustments
Stratified splitting of the dataset to ensure class balance across training, validation, and testing phases

conclusions: ConvNeXtV2 achieved the best overall performance among the tested models, attaining 88% accuracy and an F1-score of 0.87 on the HAM10000 dataset, with particular strength in melanoma detection (recall of 0.59).

ConvNeXtV2 demonstrated superior capability in handling complex cases and class imbalance compared to ResNet18, EfficientNet, and VGG16, supported by a macro AUC of 0.9764 and ROC AUC scores above 0.93 for all categories.

EfficientNet emerged as a strong alternative, achieving 87% accuracy and balanced metrics (precision 0.86, recall 0.87, F1-score 0.86), making it a reliable choice for classification tasks and deployment in scenarios with limited computing resources.

ResNet18 performed well on common benign lesions (e.g., nevi with 97% recall) but showed poor sensitivity to melanoma (recall ~0.48), indicating limitations in detecting rare but clinically relevant malignant classes.

VGG16 provided moderate overall performance (76% accuracy) and handled all classes consistently (Macro AUC 0.9491) but was outperformed by newer architectures in terms of absolute accuracy and recall scores.

The integration of Grad-CAM for explainability enhanced clinician trust and understanding by providing visual heatmaps that highlight the regions of the skin lesion influencing the model's predictions.

Deep learning models, particularly ConvNeXtV2, hold significant potential for assisting physicians in the early and accurate diagnosis of skin cancer, though challenges such as data class imbalance and generalization across diverse datasets remain.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance and demographic bias (predominantly fair-skinned populations), limiting the generalizability of the results to diverse skin types or external clinical settings.

No external validation was performed; the models were only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific distribution and lack of robustness across different imaging devices or populations.

ResNet18 demonstrated poor sensitivity to melanoma (recall ~0.48), highlighting a critical limitation in detecting malignant lesions despite strong performance on benign classes, which poses a safety risk if deployed without careful thresholding or ensemble methods.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between models, making it difficult to determine if the performance differences (e.g., ConvNeXtV2 vs. EfficientNet) are statistically significant.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while a stratified split is mentioned, the paper does not detail whether patient-level separation was enforced, which is crucial to prevent data leakage in dermatological datasets where multiple images may come from the same patient.
The batch size was dependent on the individual GPU used for training, which introduces variability in training dynamics and reproducibility across different hardware configurations.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICECONF) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may indicate less rigorous scrutiny compared to top-tier medical AI journals.

While Grad-CAM was used for explainability, the paper does not provide a quantitative or clinical validation of the explainability maps, leaving the clinical utility of the visual explanations unverified.
